C. Cunen

13 juni 2026

1 Introduction

The ACASA-TAVI study is a prospective investigation of losartan as an adjunctive therapy for patients with glioblastoma. According to Protocol Version 7.0 (17-Dec-2024), the trial collects comprehensive demographic, clinical, imaging, and quality-of-life information prior to treatment initiation.

This report summarises the currently available baseline data with emphasis on clinical status, imaging assessments, and prior treatments that may influence subsequent outcomes.

The safety analysis is based on the Safety Analysis Set including all subjects with any safety information after baseline (N=53). Patients randomised to hydroxychloroquine without receiving any amount of the treatment will be excluded from the safety population (nobody was excluded based on that condition). The primary efficacy analysis is based on the Full Analysis Set (FAS) including all randomised subjects who have had at least one baseline and one post-randomisation evaluation of efficacy (N=51). The secondary efficacy analysis is based on the Per Protocol Set including all participants of the FAS who took at least 7 of the 8 first hydroxychloroquine doses (N=49) Analysis according to final SAP version 2.1, dated 28.05.2020 Data extraction Viedoc electronic data capture system, time stamped: 26.05.2020 12:06 UTC

The aim of this document is to produce tables and figures to be included into the article as requested by email from Merete Hetland 10 December 2019. The analyses are prespecified in the Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) dated 2nd of September 2019, and the locked database with an update received on the 10th of December dated 26th of October 2019.

1.1 Randomisation Status

The present dataset is built on sham randomisation generated from the most recent export from Viedoc. No true treatment assignments have been unlocked. All findings in this document should therefore be interpreted as preliminary and descriptive only. Final efficacy comparisons will not be conducted until the database is locked and genuine randomisation codes are used.

1.2 Data preparations

The data have been further prepared in accordance with the SAP, except for the imputation of missing baseline Body Mass Index (BMI) for two trial subjects. They have been imputed with the median BMI value among all other ITT subjects. This imputation is not described in the SAP.

1.3 Specifications to the analyses

For the continuous CRP and ESR parameters, I have fitted a generalized linear mixed gamma model due to skewness. This model gave the best fit according to Akaike’s Information Criterion over a standard linear mixed model and a generalized linear mixed lognormal model. For the joint count variables I fitted a generalised linear mixed negative binomial model over a corresponding poisson model due to overdispersion. All these specification are according to the analysis plan.

2.6 Analysis sets

The FAS includes all participants. The SAS is the same as the FAS. The PP includes all participants except – non-completers that did not die (voluntary discontinuation mostly) – participants with important protocol deviations – participants switching between ASA and DOAC or stopping the treatment altogether.

Table 2.7: Number of participants in the different analysis sets.

4.1.2 Unadjusted analysis with multiple imputation

Multiple imputation with imputation model containing sex, hemoglobin, potassium, bmi, sodium and LDL cholesterol. So the model does not use the treatment variable (to stay more or less in-line with the protocol). But the analysis model only uses the treatment variable and no other covariates.

Table 4.2: Estimated average unadjusted risk difference, along with corresponding p-value and confidence interval.

contrast

estimate

SE

p.value

95CI_low

95CI_high

ASA - DOAC

-0.029

0.046

-0.121

0.062

4.1.3 Unadjusted analysis, complete-cases

Dropping participants with missing HALT value (ie. dropping 14 observations - remember that some participants with missing HALT are dead and so are counted as ‘events’ in this analysis).

4.1.4 Adjusted analysis with multilple imputation

Multiple imputation with imputation model containing sex, hemoglobin, potassium, bmi, sodium and LDL cholesterol. So the model does not use the treatment variable (to stay more or less in-line with the protocol). The analysis model uses the covariates listed above and the treatment variable in addition.

Out of the 31 safety events, 10 happen in patients that also switch between the treatments at some point. Among these 10 patients: 6 experience the safety event before switching, while 4 experience the safety event after switching.

Figure 4.2: All patients

Figure 4.3: Patients with safety events and/or switching and discontinuation

In this figure there are 46 switches and 7 discontinuations, and these number may seem to bit slightly at odds with the numbers presented in Table 2.6. But that’s actually just because what is called “switches” here in fact includes both “switches and stops” in Table 2.6.

4.2.2 Unadjusted analysis with multiple imputation, treatment policy strategy
